content.method: Legal-institutional case study analysis of Act CXC and prior negotiation episodes between the Hungarian state and church/private maintainers, combined with structured elite interviews with ministry officials, church-school administrators, and legal scholars specializing in education governance.
content.question: What is the legal and political feasibility of negotiating alignment between church/private-maintained gimnázium admissions criteria and any centrally mandated structural or admissions reform, and what do prior Hungarian reform attempts reveal about success or failure in securing such alignment?
content.why_it_matters: Every structural and admissions-based proposal (TP-live-t2, TP-live-t3, TP-live-t5) is undermined if church and private maintainers retain exemption from reformed rules, since this could preserve or even concentrate selective pathways outside state control. This is a binding constraint on the design and expected impact of nearly all reform variants.
